narrative:
The Department of Justice executed a dramatic reversal on March 3, 2026, regarding its defense of President Trump's executive orders targeting major law firms. On Monday, March 2, the DOJ filed a motion to abandon its appeals of four court rulings that had struck down Trump's executive orders against law firms Perkins Coie, WilmerHale, Susman Godfrey, and Jenner & Block [1][3][7]. However, on Tuesday, March 3, the Justice Department formally requested to withdraw its abandonment motion and continue defending the executive orders [1][3][4].

The executive orders in question were issued by Trump shortly after taking office and would have revoked security clearances for the firms' attorneys, required reviews of their government contracts, and potentially barred attorneys from entering federal government buildings [3]. The orders targeted firms "for being associated with people and supporting interests that do not align with the president or his policies" [1].

Four federal district court judges had ruled that Trump's executive orders were unconstitutional [3][7]. U.S. District Judge Beryl Howell wrote a 102-page decision in May calling the executive order against Perkins Coie "Shakespearean" [1]. The targeted firms had successfully challenged these orders in court, with federal judges consistently siding with the law firms [1].

The DOJ provided no explanation for its sudden reversal [3][7]. The department sent an email to the four firms apologizing for the short notice and informed them it would withdraw its voluntary dismissal [1]. All four law firms opposed the Justice Department's motion to continue the appeal [3][7].

Former federal district judge Shira A. Scheindlin suggested the administration's reversal might be related to concerns about losing deals with other firms, noting that while the administration would likely lose at the appeals court level, it might have "a faint hope at the Supreme Court" [1]. She characterized the reversal as "embarrassing" after "all the bad press" [1].

Nine other major law firms had previously struck deals with the White House to avoid similar executive orders, agreeing to provide almost $1 billion worth of pro bono work toward Trump's political priorities [3][8]. Paul Weiss was reportedly the first to make such a deal, arguing that $40 million in pro bono work was preferable to the potential losses from fighting the orders [8].

Critics have characterized the situation as an abuse of power. Will Creeley of the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression called Trump's actions an "indefensible vendetta" and "shocking abuse of power," stating the orders were "unconstitutional yesterday... today... and... tomorrow" [1]. Some critics referred to the settlements by other firms as acts of "capitalistic cowardice" [7].

citations.title: Trump administration reverses course on law firms
citations.quote: After asking to drop its appeal on a day earlier, the Department of Justice reversed course Tuesday saying it will continue to defend President Donald Trump's executive orders against four law firms.

citations.title: Big Law thought it won its battle with Trump. That lasted a day.
citations.quote: The Justice Department filed a notice with the US Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia on Tuesday afternoon, asking for permission to continue appealing lower court rulings that struck down Trump's executive orders.

citations.title: DOJ reverses on ending fight over Trump orders targeting law firms
citations.quote: The Justice Department has asked a federal judge to let it renew its defense of President Trump's executive orders targeting several of the nation's top law firms — a stunning reversal just a day after the government deserted the appeals.

citations.title: DoJ drops case against law firms that stood up to Trump's executive orders
citations.quote: The US justice department abruptly reversed course on Tuesday and decided it would defend executive orders made by Donald Trump to try to penalize law firms that represented clients or causes the president did not like.

citations.title: DOJ backs down in legal fight with law firms Trump targeted
citations.quote: The Department of Justice moved to dismiss its civil case against four leading law firms that won rulings against President Trump's executive orders targeting them for employing or representing his critics.

keyDevelopments: DOJ filed motion Monday to abandon appeals of court rulings against Trump's executive orders targeting law firms
DOJ reversed course Tuesday, requesting to continue defending the executive orders with no explanation provided
Four federal judges had previously ruled Trump's executive orders unconstitutional
All four targeted law firms opposed DOJ's motion to continue the appeal
Nine other law firms had previously settled with Trump administration, agreeing to nearly $1 billion in pro bono work
Friday deadline approaching for DOJ to file appellate brief defending the orders
